documents: Which writer’s name is an anagram of “I am a weakish speller”?
What is the only number spelled with the same number of letters as its value?
Which fast food chain once accepted payment in seashells on a remote island for a promotional campaign?
In Celsius and Fahrenheit, what is the one temperature that reads the same?
Which color in the hexadecimal code “#00FF00” represents maximum intensity?
Which country legally banned time travel in films unless scientifically explained?
Which food product was first advertised using the slogan “it melts in your mouth, not in your hand”?
What was the first feature-length film ever made entirely with CGI?
Which singer has a documented vocal range of more than five octaves, one of the widest ever?
Which drink was originally invented as a cure for morphine addiction?
Which bird can sleep while flying?
Which element is named after the creator of the periodic table?
What do the numbers in a barcode primarily encode?
How fast does the Earth spin at the equator?
Scientists in 2021 thawed a 24,000-year-old creature from Siberian permafrost, and it immediately began cloning itself—what microscopic animal was it?
Which 27-km-long machine, buried beneath Switzerland and France, is colder than outer space and smashes protons at nearly light speed?
Which U.S. president allegedly had to be pried out of an undersized bathtub on inauguration day, inspiring a custom tub big enough for four men?
Which Russian empress celebrated the winter of 1740 by ordering a full-scale palace made entirely of ice, complete with ice cannons that fired frozen cannonballs?
The shortest war in recorded history ended after just 38 minutes on 27 August 1896 between Britain and which sultanate?
Which land-locked South American country keeps a 5,000-strong “navy” patrolling Lake Titicaca as a reminder of the coast it lost in 1879?
Which African nation is the largest country completely surrounded by another single country?
Which country sets its clocks to UTC + 5:45, making it 15 minutes ahead of India and 15 minutes behind Bangladesh?
In which Winter Olympic event do athletes hurtle head-first down an ice track at over 130 km/h on a sled with no brakes or steering wheel?
Which bird courts mates by constructing stick “avenues” and decorating them with stolen shiny objects like bottle caps and coins?
Which Renaissance marble is the only sculpture that Michelangelo ever signed, after he overheard viewers giving the credit to someone else?
Which Dutch artist drew a lithograph of an endless staircase, later inspiring physicists to build a real water loop that appeared to flow uphill?
What is the official name for the dot above a lowercase “i” or “j,” a word that also means a tiny but crucial detail?
Which word contains all five standard vowels exactly once, in alphabetical order?
What number comes next in this sequence of U.S. states admitted to the union: 47, 48, 49…?
Which element is named after a mischievous Norse god?
Which artist painted a hyper-realistic bowl of fruit that was so detailed, birds tried to peck it?
Which mathematician’s name, when reversed, is almost “I’m a robot”?
What common English word becomes its own antonym when prefixed with “in”?
Which city is home to a library where books are shelved by color rather than subject?
Which animal has cube-shaped feces, a feature believed to help mark territory?
Which Shakespeare play has the most deaths on stage?
Which U.S. president had a pet alligator in the White House?
What is the only country whose flag is not square or rectangular?
Which muscle in the human body is, proportionally, the strongest?
Which city was originally named “Edo”?
Which singer once held a note so high it shattered a glass during a live commercial?
Which board game was banned in the Soviet Union for promoting capitalist values?
Which athlete’s name is also a unit of measurement in physics?
Which fruit was once so rare and expensive that people rented them as centerpieces?
Which mammal can survive entirely on a diet of blood?
What is the only letter not used in any U.S. state name?
Which classical composer had a catalog so large it’s numbered with “K.” followed by over 600 works?
Which historical figure is buried in three countries simultaneously due to shifting borders?
Which number is both a square and a cube (excluding 1)?
What color is the “black box” on an airplane?
What common item did NASA spend millions to develop, while Soviets just used pencils?
What is the capital of France?
What is the smallest country in the world?
Which ocean is the largest?
In what year did World War II end?
Who was the first President of the United States?
In what year did the Berlin Wall fall?
What element has the chemical symbol Au?
What planet is known as the Red Planet?
What is H2O commonly known as?
How many bones are in the human body?
Who directed the movie 'Inception'?
What year was the first Toy Story movie released?
Which band released the album 'Abbey Road'?
Who is known as the King of Pop?
How many players are on a soccer team on the field?
In which sport would you perform a slam dunk?
What does CPU stand for?
Who founded Microsoft?
At what temperature do the Celsius and Fahrenheit scales read the exact same number?
Which spice was so valuable in the 1600s that the Dutch traded the island of Manhattan to secure control of a tiny Indonesian island where it grew?
Which famously intelligent sea animal has three hearts and blue blood?
Which classic children’s modeling compound was originally developed as a wallpaper cleaner before it became a toy?
Which planet has a persistent hexagon-shaped storm pattern near its north pole?
Which number is the only one whose English name has the same number of letters as its value?
Which animal can sleep standing up by locking its legs so it doesn’t topple over?
Which country’s national flag is the only one that isn’t rectangular (it has two stacked triangles)?
Despite the name, strawberries aren’t botanically berries—yet one long yellow fruit is. Which fruit is a botanical berry?
Which famous gemstone is an electrical insulator but one of the best thermal conductors among natural materials?
Which metal can melt in your hand because its melting point is just above typical room temperature?
Bees build honeycomb cells as hexagons for efficiency — which simple reason best explains why hexagons ‘win’ here?
Roughly how much of Earth’s oxygen is produced by ocean-dwelling microorganisms like phytoplankton: closer to 10%, 30%, or 50%?
What is the only number in English whose letters are in alphabetical order?
Which iconic squishy children’s toy started life as a household product for cleaning wallpaper soot?
Which egg-laying mammal delivers venom through a spur on its hind leg?
Your phone’s GPS would drift badly without correcting for Einstein — which theory is it correcting for: special relativity, general relativity, or both?
When you eat something very spicy, why can it feel ‘hot’ even though the food isn’t high-temperature?
At what temperature do Celsius and Fahrenheit read the same number?
Which carnivorous plant snaps shut using modified leaves that act like a spring-loaded trap?

metadatas.correct_answer: The Large Hadron Collider at CERN

metadatas.correct_answer: They tile perfectly with minimal perimeter for a given area (efficient use of wax).
metadatas.alternative_answers: ["They pack space with no gaps using the least wax for the same storage area", "They tessellate without gaps while minimizing wall length"]

["It triggers the same receptors your body uses to detect heat, so your brain perceives burning"]
metadatas.correct_answer: Capsaicin activates heat/pain receptors (TRPV1), tricking nerves into signaling burning heat.
